Article.ArticleTitle: Effects of Nutrition and Exercise Interventions on Persons with Sarcopenic Obesity: An Umbrella Review of Meta-Analyses of Randomised Controlled Trials.

Abstract.AbstractText: Sarcopenic obesity (SO) is an increasing phenomenon and has been linked to several negative health consequences. The aim of this umbrella review is the assessment of effectiveness and certainty of evidence of nutrition and exercise interventions in persons with SO.

We searched for meta-analyses of RCTs in PubMed, EMBASE and CENTRAL that had been conducted in the last five years, focusing on studies on the treatment and prevention of SO. The primary endpoints were parameters for SO, such as body fat in %, skeletal muscle mass index (SMMI), gait speed, leg strength and grip strength. The methodological quality was evaluated using AMSTAR and the certainty of evidence was assessed using GRADE.

Four systematic reviews with between 30 to 225 participants were included in the umbrella review. These examined four exercise interventions, two nutrition interventions and four interventions that combined nutrition and exercise. Resistance training was the most frequently studied intervention and was found to improve gait speed by 0.14 m/s to 0.17 m/s and lower leg strength by 9.97 kg. Resistance, aerobic, mixed exercise and hypocaloric diet combined with protein supplementation is not significantly effective on selected outcomes for persons with SO compared to no intervention. The low number of primary studies included in the reviews resulted in moderate to very low certainty of evidence.

Despite the lack in certainty of evidence, resistance training may be a suitable intervention for persons with SO, in particular for improving muscle function. Nevertheless, further research is necessary to strengthen the evidence.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: This umbrella review included the systematic review by Eglseer et al. (2023) which was conducted by partially the same authors and has the same corresponding author as this umbrella review. Nevertheless, the assessment of the methodological quality of Eglseer et al. (2023) was performed on the basis of objective criteria and is in accordance with all authors. The overall rating as well as the rating for each domain is reproducible and is transparently disclosed for readers. The authors have no other competing interests to declare that are relevant to the content of this article.

Reference.Citation: St-Onge MP, Gallagher D. Body composition changes with aging: the cause or the result of alterations in metabolic rate and macronutrient oxidation? Nutrition. 2010;26(2):152–155. doi: 10.1016/j.nut.2009.07.004.
Guo SS, et al. Aging, body composition, and lifestyle: the Fels Longitudinal Study. Am J Clin Nutr. 1999;70(3):405–411. doi: 10.1093/ajcn/70.3.405.
Shilpa A, Kalyani S, Manisha S. Ageing process and physiological changes. In Gerontology, D.O. Grazia, G. Antonio, and S. Daniele, (Editors) 2018. IntechOpen: Rijeka. p. Ch. 1.
World Health Organization, Who European regional obesity report 2022. World Health Organization Copenhagen; 2022.
Batsis JA, Villareal DT. Sarcopenic obesity in older adults: aetiology, epidemiology and treatment strategies. Nat Rev Endocrinol. 2018;14(9):513–537. doi: 10.1038/s41574-018-0062-9.
Baumgartner RN, et al. Sarcopenic obesity predicts instrumental activities of daily living disability in the elderly. Obes Res. 2004;12(12):1995–2004. doi: 10.1038/oby.2004.250.
Roh E, Choi KM. Health Consequences of Sarcopenic Obesity: A Narrative Review. Front Endocrinol. 2020;11:332. doi: 10.3389/fendo.2020.00332.

•• Donini LM, et al. Definition and Diagnostic Criteria for Sarcopenic Obesity: ESPEN and EASO Consensus Statement. Obes Facts. 2022;15(3):321–335. The consensus on the definition and diagnosis of sarcopenic obesity was developed by an expert panel. The use of the consensus in future intervention studies on sarcopenic obesity can contribute to the comparability of study results and enhace the certainty in evidence regarding the effectivity of interventions aimed at persons with sarcopenic obesity.

• Gao Q, et al. Global prevalence of sarcopenic obesity in older adults: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Clin Nutr. 2021;40(7):4633–4641. The prevalence of sarcopenic obesity is dependant on which definition is used for the diagnosis. The subgroup analysis on the prevalence of sarcopenic obesity in adults over 75 years of age was higher than the overall pooled prevalence.

• Donini LM, et al. Critical appraisal of definitions and diagnostic criteria for sarcopenic obesity based on a systematic review. Clin Nutr. 2020;39(8):2368–2388. There is a wide variety of definitions and diagnostic approches on sarcopenic obesity in literature. This heterogenity affects the ability to accuralety measure the prevalence of sarcopenic obesity.

•• Schoufour JD, et al. The Relevance of Diet, Physical Activity, Exercise, and Persuasive Technology in the Prevention and Treatment of Sarcopenic Obesity in Older Adults. Front Nutr. 2021;8:661449. Refering to only nutritional interventions or only exercise interventions when preventing or treating sarcopenic obesity it not optimal. A combination of exercise and nutritonal interventions should be considered to combat sarcopenia as well as obesity. Furthermore, using health technology is a possibility to implement lifestle interventions and achieve behaviour change in persons with sarcopenic obesity in retirement age.
Bouchonville MF, Villareal DT. Sarcopenic obesity: how do we treat it? Curr Opin Endocrinol Diabetes Obes. 2013;20(5):412–419. doi: 10.1097/01.med.0000433071.11466.7f.
Villareal DT, et al. Weight loss, exercise, or both and physical function in obese older adults. N Engl J Med. 2011;364(13):1218–1229. doi: 10.1056/NEJMoa1008234.

Nabuco HCG, et al. Effect of whey protein supplementation combined with resistance training on body composition, muscular strength, functional capacity, and plasma-metabolism biomarkers in older women with sarcopenic obesity: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial. Clin Nutr ESPEN. 2019;32:88–95. doi: 10.1016/j.clnesp.2019.04.007.

Liao CD, et al. Effects of elastic band exercise on lean mass and physical capacity in older women with sarcopenic obesity: A randomized controlled trial. Sci Rep. 2018;8(1):2317. doi: 10.1038/s41598-018-20677-7.

Liao CD, et al. Effects of elastic resistance exercise on body composition and physical capacity in older women with sarcopenic obesity: A CONSORT-compliant prospective randomized controlled trial. Medicine (Baltimore) 2017;96(23):e7115. doi: 10.1097/MD.0000000000007115.

Chen HT, et al. Effects of Different Types of Exercise on Body Composition, Muscle Strength, and IGF-1 in the Elderly with Sarcopenic Obesity. J Am Geriatr Soc. 2017;65(4):827–832. doi: 10.1111/jgs.14722.

de Oliveira TMD, et al. Effects of whole-body electromyostimulation on health indicators of older people: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized trials. J Bodyw Mov Ther. 2022;31:134–145. doi: 10.1016/j.jbmt.2022.03.010.

Lau RW, et al. The effects of whole body vibration therapy on bone mineral density and leg muscle strength in older adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Clin Rehabil. 2011;25(11):975–988. doi: 10.1177/0269215511405078.

Verschueren SM, et al. The effects of whole-body vibration training and vitamin D supplementation on muscle strength, muscle mass, and bone density in institutionalized elderly women: a 6-month randomized, controlled trial. J Bone Miner Res. 2011;26(1):42–49. doi: 10.1002/jbmr.181.

Hita-Contreras F, et al. Effect of exercise alone or combined with dietary supplements on anthropometric and physical performance measures in community-dwelling elderly people with sarcopenic obesity: A meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. Maturitas. 2018;116:24–35. doi: 10.1016/j.maturitas.2018.07.007.

Yin YH, Liu JYW, Välimäki M. Effectiveness of non-pharmacological interventions on the management of sarcopenic obesity: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Exp Gerontol. 2020;135:110937. doi: 10.1016/j.exger.2020.110937.
Page MJ, et al. The PRISMA 2020 statement: An updated guideline for reporting systematic reviews. J Clin Epidemiol. 2021;134:178–189. doi: 10.1016/j.jclinepi.2021.03.001.

Shea BJ, et al. AMSTAR 2: a critical appraisal tool for systematic reviews that include randomised or non-randomised studies of healthcare interventions, or both. BMJ. 2017;358:j4008. doi: 10.1136/bmj.j4008.
Guyatt GH, et al. GRADE: an emerging consensus on rating quality of evidence and strength of recommendations. BMJ. 2008;336(7650):924–926. doi: 10.1136/bmj.39489.470347.AD.

Hsu KJ, et al. Effects of exercise and nutritional intervention on body composition, metabolic health, and physical performance in adults with sarcopenic obesity: a meta-analysis. Nutrients. 2019;11(9).

•• Eglseer D, et al. Nutritional and exercise interventions in individuals with sarcopenic obesity around retirement age: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Nutr Rev. 2023. This is the most recently published systematic review on the effectiveness of interventions aimed at adults with sarcopenic obesity. This systematic review shows that resistance training is an effective treatment for persons of retirement age with sarcopenic obesity and increased protein intake combined with exercise may increase reductions in fat mass.
Guralnik J, et al. Clinically Meaningful Change for Physical Performance: Perspectives of the ICFSR Task Force. J Frailty Aging. 2020;9(1):9–13.

• Lopez P, et al. Resistance training effectiveness on body composition and body weight outcomes in individuals with overweight and obesity across the lifespan: A systematic review and meta-analysis. Obes Rev. 2022;23(5):e13428. Resistance training is an effective intervention for weight loss in adults with obesity. The significant positive effect of resistance trainig was seen regardless of age.
Beckwée D, et al. Exercise interventions for the prevention and treatment of sarcopenia. A systematic umbrella review. J Nutr Health Aging. 2019;23(6):494–502.

Cruz-Jentoft AJ, et al. Prevalence of and interventions for sarcopenia in ageing adults: a systematic review. Report of the international sarcopenia initiative (EWGSOP and IWGS). Age Ageing. 2014;43(6):748–59.
Berger J, Fröhlich M, Kemmler W. WB-EMS Market Development—Perspectives and Threats. Int J Environ Res Public Health. 2022;19(21):14211. doi: 10.3390/ijerph192114211.
Bredella MA. Sex Differences in Body Composition. Adv Exp Med Biol. 2017;1043:9–27. doi: 10.1007/978-3-319-70178-3_2.

Ireland ML, Bolgla LA, Noehren B. Gender differences in core strength and lower extremity function during static and dynamic single-leg squat tests. ACL Injuries in the Female Athlete: Causes, Impacts, and Conditioning Programs. 2018;239–257.
Miller AEJ, et al. Gender differences in strength and muscle fiber characteristics. Eur J Appl Physiol. 1993;66(3):254–262. doi: 10.1007/BF00235103.
